script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Wednesday — here is your QQQ recap. The QQQ portfolio closed one point three five percent down on the session. It was a fractured tape, with semiconductor names taking the hardest hits and one chip giant standing apart from the rest.

Nvidia surged more than four percent while Intel cratered nearly ten. In a single session, the gap between winners and losers inside this portfolio widened dramatically — and the reasons behind that divergence carry real implications for where this index goes next.

The Nasdaq finished Wednesday's session with modest gains, driven almost entirely by artificial intelligence and large-cap tech names that held firm. The broader Dow Jones slid, weighed down by rising oil prices after President Donald Trump signaled the Iran ceasefire may be over, warning conditions would, quote, "get much worse." No specific macro data prints were available for Wednesday's session, but geopolitical tension and energy price spikes set the tone.

The top story of Wednesday's session belongs to Apple and Broadcom. Apple confirmed a multi-year chip manufacturing and co-development agreement with Broadcom valued at more than thirty billion dollars. The deal runs through twenty thirty-one and covers the production of over fifteen billion custom chips for Apple products — all manufactured in the United States. Broadcom plans a one-point-five-billion-dollar expansion of its Fort Collins, Colorado facility to support the work. For Apple, this is a deepening commitment to domestic chip sourcing at a moment when hardware and artificial intelligence architecture are converging. For Broadcom, it is a revenue anchor of historic scale. The deal is the largest chip procurement agreement Apple has ever signed with a domestic supplier. Broadcom shares jumped sharply on the news. Apple's response was more muted, gaining less than a quarter percent — suggesting the market views the deal as strategically sound for Apple but financially transformative for Broadcom. Both companies sit inside this portfolio, and the combined weight of that relationship now runs through one of the most watched supply chains in technology.

Now to the full holdings rundown.

Nvidia closed at two hundred four dollars and twelve cents, up four point three eight percent on the session. That was the standout move among the top holdings Wednesday. CNBC's Jim Cramer flagged a broader market risk in the form of rising stock offerings and debt issuance, but Nvidia's price action suggested institutional buyers were not deterred. The stock also appeared in headlines tied to artificial intelligence momentum and Nasdaq leadership. Watch for any follow-through into Thursday as the SK Hynix Nasdaq listing on July tenth approaches — that event has direct implications for the high-bandwidth memory ecosystem Nvidia depends on.

Apple closed at three hundred thirteen dollars and thirty-nine cents, up zero point two three percent on the session. The thirty-billion-dollar Broadcom chip deal was the headline, but Apple also scored a record eighty-nine Emmy Award nominations across fifteen programs, leading all networks in both Best Drama and Best Comedy categories. The combination of content strength and supply chain investment paints a broad picture of a company building across multiple fronts simultaneously. Watch Apple's response to the Broadcom deal in Thursday's session — any institutional repositioning around the partnership could move the stock more meaningfully.

Turning to Alphabet, which closed at three hundred sixty-one dollars and ninety-two cents, down one point two four percent on the session. The notable development here is that Berkshire Hathaway's new chief executive, Greg Abel, has reportedly poured more than twenty billion dollars of Berkshire's cash into Alphabet — one of the boldest bets of the post-Buffett era, according to reporting from Yahoo Finance. That kind of institutional conviction is worth noting even as the stock pulled back Wednesday. The Iran-driven risk-off tone likely pressured communication services broadly.

Over at Micron Technology, the session was painful. Shares closed at nine hundred forty-eight dollars and eighty cents, down three point six five percent. No specific company news drove the move — this looks like sector-wide pressure on memory and semiconductor names, consistent with the broader weakness in chip equipment stocks Wednesday. Micron remains a high-weight position, and back-to-back down days in this name will matter for portfolio performance.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-three dollars and thirty-four cents, down zero point eight eight percent. No material company-specific news emerged Wednesday. The move tracked the broader softness in large-cap software and cloud names. Microsoft remains the fifth-largest weight in this portfolio, and its relative stability in a down tape is worth noting — the loss was contained.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-three dollars and sixty-two cents, down zero point two two percent. One of the quieter sessions for the consumer discretionary giant. No earnings catalyst or major announcement drove the move. The mild decline in a risk-off environment suggests the stock continues to hold institutional support. Watch for any macroeconomic consumer spending data in the days ahead that could shift the narrative.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred seventeen dollars and forty cents, down six point two eight percent. That is a significant single-session loss for the third-largest semiconductor name in this portfolio. No standalone company news was identified for Wednesday, but the magnitude of the decline — more than six percent — suggests institutional selling or options-related pressure. Advanced Micro Devices has been closely correlated with artificial intelligence infrastructure sentiment, and any weakness in that theme amplifies moves quickly.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and six cents, down six point one two percent. The Iran situation and oil price spike created a complicated backdrop for the electric vehicle sector — higher energy costs can be a mixed signal for EV demand narratives. Tesla's decline mirrored Advanced Micro Devices almost point for point. No earnings or product announcements are imminent, but geopolitical volatility has historically created outsized swings in this name.

Intel closed at one hundred ten dollars and twenty-four cents, down nine point seven nine percent. That is the worst single-session performance among all primary holdings Wednesday and one of the sharpest losses in the broader index. No direct company-specific catalyst was identified in Wednesday's headlines, but Intel has faced sustained competitive pressure from both Advanced Micro Devices and Nvidia in data center and artificial intelligence chip segments. A nearly ten-percent decline without a specific news catalyst raises questions about position sizing and sector rotation dynamics inside the index.

Rounding out the primary holdings with a secondary name that generated material news — Broadcom closed at three hundred eighty-eight dollars and sixty-nine cents, up three point nine six percent. The Apple chip deal was the direct driver, as confirmed by multiple sources including Yahoo Finance. The stock jumped more than five percent intraday before settling slightly lower at close. With a one-point-five-billion-dollar facility expansion now committed, Broadcom has locked in a revenue stream that extends to twenty thirty-one.

Three things to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, the SK Hynix Nasdaq listing is scheduled for July tenth — reported to be the largest American depositary receipt offering in market history, with direct ties to high-bandwidth memory and Nvidia's artificial intelligence supply chain. Second, monitor oil prices and any further statements from the Trump administration on Iran — energy spikes have already pressured the Dow and could rotate capital away from growth names. Third, watch Intel for any institutional commentary or analyst revisions following Wednesday's nearly ten-percent decline — a move of that size without a clear catalyst often precedes a formal response from the sell side.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. The SK Hynix listing Thursday and continued Iran-driven oil volatility will be the two pressure points most worth watching as this portfolio looks to recover Wednesday's losses.
